item.title: Pantry match
item.description: List the ingredients you have. See which recipes are within reach — and which are one or two shopping items away.

item.title: Ají amarillo paste
item.description: The blended paste of the Peruvian yellow chili (Capsicum baccatum). Fruity, moderately hot, the defining flavor of Peruvian cooking.

item.title: Ají panca paste
item.description: The dried-and-blended paste of the deep-red Peruvian chili (Capsicum baccatum). Smoky, mild, raisin-sweet. The marinade base of anticuchos.

item.title: Allspice (pimento)
item.description: The dried unripe berry of Pimenta dioica, a Jamaican evergreen. Tastes like cinnamon, clove, and nutmeg combined into one berry. The defining spice of Jamaican jerk.

item.title: Almonds
item.description: Prunus dulcis. The thinning-and-thickening nut of Mediterranean, Iberian, Middle Eastern, and Mughlai cooking. Used whole, slivered, ground (almond flour), or as the structural emulsifier in almond-based sauces (picada, salsa de almendras).

item.title: Bagoong
item.description: Filipino fermented shrimp or fish paste. Two main forms: bagoong alamang (pink shrimp paste, wet) and bagoong isda (fish paste, brown). Eaten raw with green mango or sauteed.

item.title: Banana ketchup
item.description: Filipino condiment of mashed banana, vinegar, sugar, and spices, tinted red to look like tomato ketchup. World War II improvisation that became a real cuisine staple.

item.title: Banh trang (Vietnamese rice paper)
item.description: Thin, translucent sheets of rice flour and tapioca starch, dried on bamboo mats. The wrapper for goi cuon (fresh rolls) and cha gio (fried rolls). Activated by warm-water dip.

item.title: Beef brisket
item.description: The breast section of the steer, a heavily-worked muscle with significant connective tissue. The canonical cut for long slow braises, smoked barbecue, and pot roasts.

item.title: Beef heart
item.description: The heart muscle of the steer. Lean, dense, mineral-rich; structurally an unusually tender working muscle. The canonical protein for Peruvian anticuchos.

item.title: Berbere
item.description: Ethiopian chili-and-warm-spice blend. Foundational seasoning for every wat (stew) and several dry rubs across Ethiopian and Eritrean cooking.

item.title: Calamansi
item.description: Small Philippine citrus, a lime-mandarin hybrid. Greenish skin, orange flesh, intensely tart with a floral-mandarin top note. The defining acid of Filipino cooking.

item.title: Candlenut (kemiri)
item.description: Waxy, off-white nut from the candlenut tree, related to macadamia. Used pounded into spice pastes as a thickener and fat carrier. Mildly toxic raw, always cooked.

item.title: Caraway seed
item.description: Seed of Carum carvi, a biennial in the parsley family. Anise-leaning warm-bitter aroma from carvone, the defining note of rye bread, sauerkraut, and many Central and Northern European preparations.

item.title: Dendê oil (red palm oil)
item.description: Unrefined red palm oil from the fruit of Elaeis guineensis. Bright orange-red, savory, the structural fat of Bahian Brazilian and West African cooking.

item.title: Dill (fresh)
item.description: Feathery green leaves of Anethum graveolens. Bright, grassy, faintly anise-leaning aroma. A primary herb in Northern, Central, and Eastern European cooking, distinct from a garnish.

item.title: Dried porcini
item.description: Dehydrated Boletus edulis caps. Concentrated umami, woody-earthy aroma, the canonical dried mushroom of Italian, French, and Central European cooking. The soaking liquid is structural.

item.title: Dry sherry (Fino or Manzanilla)
item.description: Fortified wine from Jerez, Spain, aged under flor yeast. Bone-dry, briny, and intensely savory. The structural deglazing liquid of Spanish cooking and an under-recognized umami accelerant.

item.title: Egusi seeds
item.description: Shelled, dried melon seeds (Cucumeropsis or Citrullus species) used ground as the structural thickener in West African egusi soup. Nutty, fatty, mildly bitter, ~50% oil by weight.

item.title: Galangal
item.description: Pale rhizome of Alpinia galanga, related to ginger but flavorally distinct. Piney, citrusy, with a faint medicinal eucalyptus note. Not substitutable with ginger.

item.title: Green cabbage
item.description: Brassica oleracea var. capitata. The tight-headed pale-green cabbage of European and American supermarkets. Structural in slaws, ferments, braises, stuffed-cabbage preparations, and many soups.

item.title: Green plantain
item.description: Unripe Musa paradisiaca, starchy and astringent. The structural starch of West African and Caribbean cooking.

item.title: Hoisin sauce
item.description: Thick, sweet, fermented soybean sauce with garlic, vinegar, chili, and five-spice. The defining condiment of Cantonese roast meats and Southern Vietnamese pho garnish.

item.title: Holy basil (bai kaprao)
item.description: Peppery, clove-forward basil with serrated leaves and green or purple stems. The canonical herb of Thai pad kaprao. Not interchangeable with Thai basil.

item.title: Hominy (nixtamal)
item.description: Whole maize kernels processed with alkali to remove the seed coat and gelatinize the starch. The structural grain of pozole and many Mesoamerican corn dishes.

item.title: Hungarian paprika
item.description: Ground dried sweet pepper from Hungary, the defining spice of Hungarian cooking. Bright, fruity, low-heat to medium-heat depending on grade. Not interchangeable with Spanish pimentón.

item.title: Jalapeño
item.description: Capsicum annuum. Medium-heat green chile pepper, the everyday Mexican and Tex-Mex chile. Used raw in salsas, pickled in escabeche, smoked-and-dried to become chipotle.

item.title: Kaffir lime leaves (makrut lime leaves)
item.description: Glossy, double-lobed leaves of Citrus hystrix. Floral citrus aroma from citronellal, bruised whole in broths or shredded fine in salads. No real substitute.

item.title: Kecap manis
item.description: Indonesian sweet soy sauce. Thick, syrupy, sweetened with palm sugar and aromatized with star anise and galangal. The defining condiment of Javanese cooking.

item.title: Lard (manteca)
item.description: Rendered pork fat. The traditional cooking fat of Mexican, Filipino, Eastern European, and Southern American cooking. Neutral-tasting, high smoke point, structurally tender for pastry.
